- [x] [Tests added and passed](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/dev/development/contributing_codebase.html#writing-tests) if fixing a bug or adding a new feature
- [x] Added an entry in the latest `doc/source/whatsnew/v3.1.0.rst` file if fixing a bug or adding a new feature.

`DataFrame.melt` already rejects `value_name` values that collide with an existing column. There was no analogous check for `var_name`. When the resolved `var_name` collided with an `id_var` label, with `value_name`, or duplicated entries inside a list-like `var_name`, `melt` returned a frame with duplicate output columns and silently overwrote `id_vars` data.
ValueError: var_name produces duplicate output column names with id_vars

After `var_name` is normalized to a list, build the output-name sequence (`id_vars + var_name + [value_name]`) and raise `ValueError` if it contains duplicates. The error message lists which labels collided. Works for both scalar and MultiIndex column inputs.
Three new regression tests in `pandas/tests/reshape/test_melt.py`, mirroring the existing `test_raise_of_column_name_value` style:
Smoke-verified against current behavior using the installed pandas with the patched `melt` sideloaded — all existing happy-path melt calls still work; only the three error cases now raise.

This fixes chained `pathlib.Path / Series / Series` operations when the string suffixes are backed by PyArrow. Arrow string arithmetic now handles aligned arrays of `Path` values in the same object-result path used for scalar `Path` division, avoiding PyArrow coercion of `Path` objects.

{'id': 219960758, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMTk5NjA3NTg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/labels/Needs%20Discussion', 'name': 'Needs Discussion', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False, 'description': 'Requires discussion from core team before further action'}]",open,False,[],,0,2026-05-22T16:33:03Z,2026-05-22T16:33:03Z,,,MEMBER,[],,,,,"Historically, by using `pytz` by default for time zones, tz-aware data had wrong offsets for DST periods after 2038 (e.g. see https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/33061 and https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/28207, and documented at https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/3.0/user_guide/timeseries.html#working-with-time-zones (scroll down to the 5th(!) warning box), sidenote: we should update this).

The same happens if you were using `dateutil.tz` time zones, and the short summary is that pytz and dateutil are only parsing the Version 1 TZif files, those don't include a ""rule"" for future times, and therefore typically include transitions times extrapolated to the future, but because of 32bit are limited to dates up to 2038.

This issue was fixed by switching to `zoneinfo` by default (https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/34916) in pandas 3.0, since zoneinfo supports the newer versions of TZif files, and can use a rule-based logic for determining the offset for distant dates.

However, then after improving the performance for `zoneinfo` timezones to also use our internal fast binary search of the transition points (https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/64379) in pandas 3.0.3, this changed this behaviour again, because now we have to pre-populate our array of transition dates (to use for the binary search), and the linked PR decided to do that up to 2100 (meaning we only get correct offsets up to 2100, and not for later dates, see the example below).
In https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/65705, I am extending that cut-off from 2100 to 2262.
While having tz-aware data so far in the future is a bit a debatable use case, having an arbitrary cut off after which it behaves differently is not ideal.
- Decide we are OK with an arbitrary cut-off if it is far enough in the future (e.g. 2262) and document this

- Populate the transition data dynamically based on the range of the input data (eg by default fill up to year 2100, but if the input data max year is 2150, we recalculate it up to that date and update the cached data)
- Use our fast-path for a ""normal"" range (eg up to 2100) to cover most use cases, and then for dates after that, use the `zoneinfo` python API directly as a slower fallback

`DataFrame.any(skipna=False)` and `DataFrame.all(skipna=False)` raise `ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to bool` on numpy-nullable columns (`boolean`, `Int*`, `UInt*`, `Float*`) whenever the reduction would propagate `pd.NA`.
The corresponding `Series.any/all(skipna=False)` reductions on the same data correctly return `pd.NA`.

`DataFrame.any(skipna=False)` and `DataFrame.all(skipna=False)` should return a `Series` with nullable boolean dtype (`boolean`) containing `pd.NA` when the corresponding `Series.any/all(skipna=False)` reduction on the same column returns `pd.NA`.

b & [pd.NA, False]   # TypeError: boolean value of NA is ambiguous

column2: `, `^`) work with a scalar `pd.NA` but raise a confusing

column1: low-level `""boolean value of NA is ambiguous""` error when the right operand is a
dtype-less sequence (e.g. `list`/`tuple`) that contains `pd.NA`. A dtype-less sequence
of plain bools (`b & [True, False]`) was instead silently accepted, while the
equivalent operation on a `Series` already raises a clear error.
In `BooleanArray._logical_method`, a list-like operand is coerced with
`np.asarray(other, dtype=""bool"")`. When the sequence contains `pd.NA`, NumPy evaluates
`bool(pd.NA)`, which raises `TypeError: boolean value of NA is ambiguous`. The
numpy-backed `Series` path (`ops.array_ops.logical_op`) instead raises a dedicated,
Align `BooleanArray` logical ops with the `Series` behavior: raise the same
`TypeError` for dtype-less sequences, matching the direction agreed on in the issue
and in the previous (stale) PR discussion (#63127):
# TypeError: Logical ops (and, or, xor) between Pandas objects and dtype-less
# sequences (e.g. list, tuple) are no longer supported. Wrap the object in a
# Series, Index, or np.array before operating instead.

Fixes a regression in `Index.str.findall` introduced in recent 3.1.0 development versions.
because the result wrapping path attempted to construct an `Index` directly from nested Python lists, triggering stricter hashability validation introduced in newer development versions.
This PR restores the previous behavior by preserving list-like object results correctly when wrapping the result into an `Index`.
* preservation of object-dtype nested list outputs
* [x] Reviewed and followed the contribution guidelines

AI-assisted tooling (GPT 5.5) was used for guidance and debugging support while developing this PR.",,"{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65692/reactions', 'total_count': 0, '+1': 0, '-1': 0, 'laugh': 0, 'hooray': 0, 'confused': 0, 'heart': 0, 'rocket': 0, 'eyes': 0}",https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/65692/timeline,,,,,

As discussed, this Proof of Concept introduces the `assert_cow_inplace_safe` utility in `pandas/tests/copy_view/test_external_inplace.py`.
The goal of this utility is to validate that external C-engine/NumExpr operations do not strip CoW tracking metadata when `inplace=True` is invoked.
I have wired it up to `DataFrame.query()` and `DataFrame.where()` as our initial targets to establish the perimeter.

Error: The process '/home/runner/.pixi/bin/pixi' failed with exit code 1

The pyarrow-nightly environment uses the scientific-python nightly wheel index. However, pixi.lock pins a direct URL for the pyarrow-25.0.0.dev48 wheel, and that nightly artifact has been removed from the index, causing a 404 during installation.
The pyarrow-nightly CI job should not fail simply because an older nightly wheel artifact has been pruned.

This PR addresses the tracker at Issue #63207 for DataFrame.drop()
It adds a deprecation warning (Pandas4Warning) for DataFrame.drop(), a deprecation note in docstring, a test for the warning, edits to other affected test files, and whatsnew update.
Adding this warning to DataFrame.drop affected quite a few tests (about 1000 initially failed), so I did 3 things:
1) Added filterwarning decorator to filter out the warning for tests to several test files.
2) Edited _drop_labels_or_levels in generic.py to not use inplace in 2 instances. Other methods (melt and merge) call this and it affected their tests.

3) There was one particular test (test_inplace_drop_and_operation in test_drop.py) where adding the pytest.mark.filterwarning decorator did not filter out the warning, and I made changes to the test itself, which I was trying to avoid. Please let me know if that's not the correct way to address this.
I ran pytest pandas -n 4 -m ""not slow and not network and not db and not single_cpu"" -r sxX.

The result are the same 7 failures I also get when running that same command on my local main branch (no diffs to upstream). While investigating a memory corruption issue yesterday (reported in #65664  and patched in PR #65675 ), I found a structural vulnerability in how the computation engine interacts with Pandas 3.0's Copy-on-Write (CoW) architecture.

Specifically, `df.eval(""new=old"", inplace=True)` was bypassing CoW protections. The `eval` engine reached into the `BlockManager`, extracted the raw data arrays, evaluated them, and handed them back. Because the data took a detour through the external computation engine, the CoW reference tracking metadata was stripped. The DataFrame assumed the returned array was unshared memory, leading to silent memory corruption when the alias was later modified.

My patch forces an explicit `.copy()` before assignment if `inplace=True` inside `pandas.core.computation.eval`. However, this raises a much larger architectural question: **Are there other C-engine, Cython, or NumExpr-routed methods that are secretly leaking CoW tracking data when `inplace=True` is invoked?**

We know that standard `__setitem__` handles CoW perfectly. But any method that extracts raw arrays, computes externally, and attempts to write back `inplace` is at risk. I propose a systematic audit of the following `inplace=True` methods to ensure they aren't making the same tracking error:

1. **`DataFrame.query(inplace=True)`:** Since this relies on the exact same underlying `pandas.core.computation` engines (`numexpr` / `python`) as `eval`, it is the most likely candidate for a parallel vulnerability.
2. **`DataFrame.where()` & `DataFrame.mask()`:** When backed by external engines or fast-path Cython blocks.
3. **`DataFrame.fillna()` & `DataFrame.replace()`:** Specifically when operating on mixed dtypes that might force a block consolidation or split during the inplace write.
Rather than playing whack-a-mole with memory bugs, I suggest we:
1. Identify all `inplace=True` paths that route through `numexpr` or bypass the standard `NDFrame.__setitem__` API.
2. Implement a standardized CoW ""alias corruption"" test suite specifically targeting these external engine routes.
3. Determine if we need a centralized validation wrapper that explicitly checks reference counts when C-engines return data for inplace assignment.

I am happy to lead the audit on `.query()` and `.where()` to start, but wanted to bring this to the core team's attention to see if there is already a broader strategy for validating `inplace` safety under CoW. Thoughts?
I propose implementing a two-phase solution to secure the CoW perimeter:
Introduce a new test utility in `pandas/tests/copy_view/` specifically designed to test external-engine mutations.
Ensures that an inplace operation does not corrupt aliases
if it routes through an external engine.
# Perform inplace operation that might strip CoW tracking
# If the original dataframe was mutated, the CoW perimeter failed.

If the audit finds multiple leaks in .query(), .where(), etc., rather than patching them individually, we should implement a validation layer inside BlockManager.setitem_inplace (or the equivalent API) that explicitly asserts: if pd.options.mode.copy_on_write: assert result.refs is safe before mapping the C-engine memory back to the dataframe.
We do nothing structurally, and wait for users to report data corruption bugs one by one as they upgrade to Pandas 3.0. This risks user trust in the new CoW architecture.
**Alternative 2: Do nothing and wait for PDEP-8**

Since `inplace=True` is broadly slated for deprecation and removal in Pandas 4.0 (PDEP-8), we could argue that investing in `inplace` infrastructure isn't worth the time. *However*, because users will be relying on Pandas 3.x during the CoW transition period, allowing silent data corruption to exist during this window is highly dangerous. We need to secure the perimeter until `inplace` is officially removed.
This architectural concern was triggered by my investigation into Issue #65664, where I successfully patched a live CoW tracking bypass in `DataFrame.eval(inplace=True)` via PR #65675.
